perturbation.original_text: During the period from acceptance of this agreement until the earlier of (a): the execution of a definitive agreement or (b) the date that is 45 days after the date of such acceptance, the Company and the officers, directors, employees and other representatives of the Company including JPM shall not directly or indirectly, solicit, initiate, or encourage any offers or proposals for the acquisition of the Company, or of any of the capital stock or all, or substantially all, of the assets of the Company, from third parties (an "Acquisition Proposal").
perturbation.changed_text: During the period from acceptance of this agreement until the earlier of (a): the execution of a definitive agreement or (b) the date that is 15 days after the date of such acceptance, the Company and the officers, directors, employees and other representatives of the Company including JPM shall not directly or indirectly, solicit, initiate, or encourage any offers or proposals for the acquisition of the Company, or of any of the capital stock or all, or substantially all, of the assets of the Company, from third parties (an "Acquisition Proposal").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the "no shop" period to 15 days could be deemed an unreasonably short period under Delaware law (which often governs M&A transactions), potentially violating the board's fiduciary duties to obtain the best possible price for shareholders. While NY law governs this agreement, the underlying transaction is an acquisition, making the law relevant.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law, Section 141(a) and related case law regarding fiduciary duties of directors in M&A transactions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law (8 Del. C. § 141(a)) vests the board of directors with the authority to manage the corporation. Case law interpreting this section establishes that directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of the shareholders, including maximizing value in a sale transaction. A 15-day 'no-shop' period may be insufficient for the board to adequately explore other potential offers, potentially breaching their fiduciary duty. While this agreement is under NY law, challenges related to M&A can involve Delaware law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. perturbation.justification: The original text provides a 45-day period during which the Company is restricted from soliciting acquisition proposals. The changed text reduces this 'no-shop' period to 15 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this shorter period could be deemed unreasonably short under Delaware law, potentially violating the board's fiduciary duties. The Law Explanation expands on this, stating directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of shareholders, including maximizing value in a sale transaction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 8 Del. C. § 141, indicates that 'the business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors.' This is interpreted by case law as establishing a fiduciary duty. A 15-day 'no-shop' period, as explained, may be insufficient for the board to adequately explore other potential offers, creating a conflict with this duty. Therefore, the modified text creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating fiduciary duties as understood under Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: Legal Remedy ------------ We understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Legal Remedy ------------ We understand and agree that money damages would be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company will ONLY be entitled to monetary damages as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and it excludes all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the remedy solely to monetary damages and excluding specific performance and injunctive relief is against basic principles of contract remedies under New York law. Specific performance is an option.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law allows for specific performance as a remedy under certain circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 7109 allows for specific performance in some circumstances. The agreement's limitation to monetary damages only would override this. In NY, barring equitable remedies and limiting the company to damages only is potentially unenforceable because it seeks to exclude the equitable remedies that a court can consider. This could be deemed unconscionable or against public policy depending on the breach.

Unique chattel. (a) Injunction, temporary restraining order. Where the chattel is unique, the court may grant a preliminary injunction or temporary restraining order that the chattel shall not be removed from the state, transferred, sold, pledged, assigned or otherwise disposed of until the further order of the court. (b) Judgment enforceable by contempt. Where the chattel is unique, the court, in addition to granting a judgment under section 7108, may direct that a party in possession deliver the chattel to the party entitled to possession. Disobedience of a judgment or order so directing may be punished as a contempt of court. If a party accepts the value of the chattel awarded to him by the judgment, he shall have no claim to the chattel.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts established legal principles regarding remedies for breach of contract in New York. The original text allows for specific performance and injunctive relief, in addition to monetary damages. The changed text limits the remedy solely to monetary damages, explicitly excluding all other remedies. This is a significant departure because, under certain circumstances, New York law, specifically CPLR § 7109, allows for specific performance, especially when dealing with 'unique chattel.' While the snippets focus on 'unique chattel,' the Law Explanation suggests the principle extends more broadly, making the limitation to monetary damages potentially unenforceable and against public policy. By removing the possibility of specific performance and injunctive relief, the changed text undermines the court's ability to enforce the contract fully and provide equitable remedies, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties hereto regarding the subject matter hereof. This Agreement may be changed only by a written agreement signed by the parties hereto or their authorized representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties hereto regarding the subject matter hereof. This Agreement may be changed only by an oral agreement between the parties hereto or their authorized representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the clause to allow oral modifications is problematic because it contradicts the Statute of Frauds in New York, which requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Statute of Frauds, General Obligations Law § 5-701
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Gen. Oblig. Law § 5-701 requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. By allowing oral modifications, the agreement becomes vulnerable to unenforceability under the Statute of Frauds if the modification falls under the types of agreements that must be in writing, such as agreements that cannot be performed within one year.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The modification allowing oral agreements directly contradicts NY Gen. Oblig. Law § 5-701, which requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states: 'Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith...if such agreement...by its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof'. The changed text introduces a direct conflict with this requirement, as it allows for agreements that should be in writing to be made orally, rendering them potentially void and unenforceable.
